user_query:
Set up a complete SEO alert system for our domain novametrics.io. Please read the following inputs under input/ and build a threshold- and priority-based alerting framework:

- input/baseline_metrics.csv — 12 weeks of baselines for rankings, traffic, technical metrics, backlinks, and AI visibility. Columns: date, category (Rankings|Traffic|Technical|Backlinks|GEO|Brand), metric, scope (e.g., keyword: "project management software"; page: "/pricing"; or "sitewide"), value, units, data_source, collected_at.
- input/critical_keywords.json — Array with objects including keyword, current_rank, tier (1/2/3), page_url, and notes.
- input/notification_prefs.yaml — Roles, channels, preferences, office hours, suppression, and escalation preferences. Example roles: SEO Manager, Dev Team, Marketing Lead, Executive.

Deliverables (write all files under output/ with these exact paths):
1) output/alert_config.md — A human-readable configuration document that includes:
   - "SEO Alert System Configuration" header with domain and configured date
   - An "Alert Categories" table listing Rankings, Traffic, Technical, Backlinks, Competitors, GEO, and Brand with descriptions and typical urgency
   - For each category, 3–6 concrete alert rules with trigger condition, threshold, and priority
   - A "Response Plans by Priority" section with time-bound actions for Critical, High, Medium, and Low
   - A "Notification Setup" section reflecting notification_prefs.yaml, including channels by priority, role-based routing, suppression windows, and escalation steps
   - A "Weekly Alert Review Checklist"
   - In every alert table row, cite data_source and collected_at date for the baseline used

2) output/alert_rules.json — A machine-readable rules file with:
   - Top-level keys: domain (string), configured_date (ISO 8601), notification_channels (object mapping priority to array of channels), recipients (object mapping roles to which priorities they receive), rules (array)
   - rules[*] must include: category (one of Rankings, Traffic, Technical, Backlinks, Competitors, GEO, Brand), name, metric, condition, threshold_method (stddev|percentage|absolute), threshold (object with clearly labeled values), priority (Critical|High|Medium|Low|Positive permitted), data_source (string), baseline_source (string)
   - For rules using stddev, include a baseline object with mean and stddev numbers; for percentage, include comparison_period (e.g., DoD, WoW, MoM); for absolute, include units
   - Include notification suppression (cooldown mins) at the rule level where relevant
   - Ensure the rules array includes at least one rule per category and at least 20 total rules

3) output/alert_summary.csv — A summary table with headers: Category,Critical,High,Medium,Low,Total and one row each for Rankings, Traffic, Technical, Backlinks, Competitors, GEO, Brand, plus a final Total row. Counts should match the number of rules per category and priority in output/alert_rules.json.

Methodology and constraints:
- Use the baseline data to set thresholds. For continuous/volatile metrics, prefer the standard deviation method. For metrics like WoW traffic or page-level declines, use percentage thresholds. For binary/count events (e.g., security issues, SSL expiry, 5xx errors), use absolute thresholds.
- Incorporate tiers from input/critical_keywords.json to set tighter thresholds for Tier 1 keywords and more permissive thresholds for Tier 2/3.
- Map priority to response times and tie technical alerts to Dev Team routing. Respect office hours, suppression rules (e.g., duplicate cooldowns), and escalation from notification_prefs.yaml.
- If any input is missing or incomplete, note reasonable assumptions in output/alert_config.md and still produce a coherent, production-ready configuration.

Please write the three files to:
- output/alert_config.md
- output/alert_rules.json
- output/alert_summary.csv

Double-check that the summary counts match the JSON rules and that all seven categories are covered.
